title: Quantum computing with noninteracting particles
speaker: Alex Arkhipov
abstract: We introduce an abstract model of computation corresponding to an experiment in which identical, non-interacting bosons are sent through a non-adaptive linear circuit before being measured. We show that despite the very limited nature of the model, an exact classical simulation would imply a collapse of the polynomial hierarchy. Moreover, under plausible conjectures, a "noisy" approximate simulation would do the same.

proof_stages.label: Quantum-supremacy motivation for a weak model
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames quantum computing against the extended Church-Turing thesis and motivates noninteracting particles as a deliberately weak, likely nonuniversal model that may still cross the classical/quantum boundary.

proof_stages.label: Classical noninteracting particles yield permanents
proof_stages.summary: A classical balls-and-slots model is defined by a one-particle stochastic matrix, and indistinguishability plus independence makes multi-particle transition probabilities permanents of generalized submatrices.

proof_stages.label: Quantum amplitudes: bosonic permanents and fermionic determinants
proof_stages.summary: Replacing stochastic probabilities by unitary amplitudes gives bosonic transition probabilities as squared moduli of permanents and fermionic transition probabilities as squared moduli of determinants, allowing interference cancellations absent classically.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic formalism for indistinguishability and interference
proof_stages.summary: Configurations are encoded as monomials in commuting bosonic or anticommuting fermionic variables, making normalization, Pauli exclusion, and cancellation-driven correlations transparent.

proof_stages.label: Sampling-problem formulation and easy cases
proof_stages.summary: Boson sampling and fermion sampling are formulated as randomized computational problems, and the speaker explains why fermion sampling and classical particle sampling are efficiently simulable despite exponentially many outcomes.

proof_stages.label: Boson-sampling hardness from permanents
proof_stages.summary: Using the #P-hardness of permanents and the ability to embed matrices into unitaries, the main result is stated: exact boson sampling is not in BPP unless the polynomial hierarchy collapses, with approximate hardness conditional on further conjectures.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and comparison with universal quantum algorithms
proof_stages.summary: The hardness theorem is interpreted as evidence for quantum supremacy using only a one-step noninteracting process, and its assumptions, usefulness, verifiability, and robustness are compared with Shor-style quantum algorithms.

proof_stages.label: Experimental status and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains the linear-optical implementation via beam splitters and phase shifters, reports small-photon experiments and target scales, and closes with open problems about decision versions, usefulness, verification, and scalable devices.

examples.name: Classical computer simulating water waves
examples.note: The water-wave analogy illustrates why quantum supremacy is a computational simulation claim rather than the trivial observation that a classical computer is not physically quantum.
examples.engagement_reason: used only as an analogy that ordinary computers can simulate wave dynamics despite lacking water

examples.name: Two-boson Hadamard / Hong-Ou-Mandel dip
examples.note: The two-photon Hadamard calculation shows directly how commuting bosonic variables create destructive interference of the split outcomes, yielding the key intuition for noninteracting yet correlated bosons.
examples.engagement_reason: runs two bosons through the Hadamard matrix, expands the variables, shows cross-term cancellation, and derives the Hong-Ou-Mandel probabilities

passes.reason: balanced Hadamard beam splitter gives the extreme Hong-Ou-Mandel case of perfect bosonic bunching
The two-boson Hadamard/Hong-Ou-Mandel setup is a balanced highly regular case with perfect bunching and zero coincidence probability.
the balanced two-boson Hadamard case is a symmetric perfect-bunching extreme that defeats the independent-particle intuition
The balanced Hadamard beam-splitter setup is symmetric and gives the extremal Hong-Ou-Mandel zero-coincidence dip, showing noninteracting identical bosons need not behave independently.
The balanced two-mode Hadamard boson setup is highly symmetric and gives the extremal Hong-Ou-Mandel zero-coincidence/perfect-bunching case.

The two-boson Hadamard/Hong-Ou-Mandel setup is a balanced symmetric two-mode case exhibiting perfect, hence extremal, bosonic bunching.
